model_narrative: We plan the management of a regional forest economy over a sequence of periods. In each period we decide how much existing forest to cut and how much new forest to plant and later manage, by species and site class; how many logs of each type to deliver to industry; how intensely to run each wood-processing activity; how much new mill capacity to build; and how many units of each final product to ship to market. Each period also carries its own bookkeeping figures for investment, processing, cutting and planting costs and for sales revenue. The objective is to maximize the total discounted net benefit over the whole horizon.

sets.name: c
sets.doc: all commodities in the wood economy, spanning both intermediate logs and final products

sets.name: cf
sets.doc: the final products that are sold to market; a subset of the commodities

sets.name: cl
sets.doc: the log types harvested from the forest and fed to industry; a subset of the commodities

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the tree species grown

sets.name: k
sets.doc: site classes describing land quality

sets.name: at
sets.doc: tree age classes of the existing forest, in years

sets.name: u
sets.doc: the initial age class of an existing stand at the start of the horizon; ranges over the same age classes

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the wood-processing activities (processes) that convert log inputs into products

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the productive units (mills) whose capacity must be built and respected

sets.name: te
sets.doc: the extended planning horizon of periods in chronological order; the first member is the opening period

sets.name: t
sets.doc: the model decision horizon of periods, in chronological order; here it coincides with the extended horizon

sets.name: wpos
sets.members: sparse set of feasible (initial-age, period) pairs
sets.doc: the set of feasible (initial age, period) pairs at which an existing stand of that starting age may be cut; only these combinations are admissible

sets.name: vpos
sets.members: sparse set of feasible (planting-period, period) pairs
sets.doc: the set of feasible (planting period, later period) pairs; a stand planted in the first period of the pair can be managed or cut in the second; only these combinations are admissible

params.name: mup
params.doc: the planting cost, in US dollars per hectare

params.name: muc
params.doc: the cutting cost, in US dollars per cubic metre of logs

params.name: sgm
params.doc: the capital recovery factor applied to convert built capacity into an annualized investment charge

params.name: scd
params.doc: the fraction of land falling in each site class; a distribution over site classes

params.name: land
params.doc: the land available for each species, in thousands of hectares

params.name: pc
params.doc: the unit process cost of each activity, in US dollars per cubic metre of input

params.name: pd
params.doc: the sales price of each final product, in US dollars per unit

params.name: nu
params.doc: the investment cost of building one unit of capacity at each mill, in US dollars per cubic metre of input

params.name: delt
params.doc: the discount factor applied to each period's net benefit

params.name: a
params.doc: the input-output matrix: per unit of process activity, the amount of each commodity produced (positive) or consumed (negative)

params.name: b
params.doc: the capacity utilization coefficient: input of mill capacity consumed per unit of each process activity

params.name: avl
params.doc: an availability mask over (period, period) pairs that is nonzero exactly when capacity built in the second period is live and usable in the first period; capacity once built stays available

params.name: iad
params.doc: the initial age distribution: the proportion of each species' existing forest that starts in each age class

params.name: yw
params.doc: the per-hectare yield of each log type from cutting existing forest of a given starting age and species in a given period, in cubic metres per hectare

params.name: yv
params.doc: the per-hectare yield of each log type from managed (newly planted) forest, indexed by planting period, cutting period, species, log type, and site class, in cubic metres per hectare

vars.name: w
vars.doc: the area of existing forest of a given species and site class, starting age and period, that is cut, in thousands of hectares per year; defined only over feasible (initial-age, period) pairs

vars.name: v
vars.doc: the area of managed new forest of a given species and site class, planted in one period and acted on in a later period, in thousands of hectares per year; defined only over feasible (planting-period, period) pairs

vars.name: r
vars.doc: the supply of each log type delivered to industry in each period, in thousands of cubic metres per year

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the activity level of each process in each period, in thousands of cubic metres of input per year

vars.name: h
vars.doc: the capacity expansion built at each mill in each period, in thousands of cubic metres of input per year

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the final shipments of each commodity to market in each period, in thousands of units per year

vars.name: phik
vars.doc: the investment cost incurred in each period, in thousands of US dollars per year

vars.name: phir
vars.doc: the process cost incurred in each period, in thousands of US dollars per year

vars.name: phix
vars.doc: the sales revenue earned in each period, in thousands of US dollars per year

vars.name: phil
vars.doc: the cutting cost incurred in each period, in thousands of US dollars per year

vars.name: phip
vars.doc: the planting cost incurred in each period, in thousands of US dollars per year

vars.name: phi
vars.doc: the total discounted net benefit over the whole horizon

constraints.name: wpos)
constraints.intent: The amount of existing forest that gets cut over the whole horizon cannot exceed how much existing forest there is to begin with. For each species, site class, and starting age class, the total area of that existing stand cut across all the periods where cutting it is admissible must stay within the area of that stand initially present, which is the species' available land scaled by the share of land in that site class and the share of the forest that starts in that age class.

constraints.name: wpos)
constraints.intent: New forest cannot be managed before it exists. For each species, site class, and period, the area of newly planted forest that is acted on in that period cannot exceed the area that has been established by then, namely the existing forest cut in that period plus the new forest planted in earlier periods that becomes available in that period.

constraints.name: wpos)
constraints.intent: The logs delivered to industry must come from what is actually harvested. For each log type and each period, the supply of that log type delivered equals the total yield obtained from cutting, summed over both the managed newly planted forest and the existing forest, across all species, site classes, and source stands feasible for that period, each area multiplied by its per-hectare yield.

constraints.name: te)
constraints.intent: Log supply to industry should never fall over time, so harvests are sustained. For each log type, the supply delivered in a period must be at least the supply delivered in the immediately preceding period. The very first period has no predecessor and so carries no such requirement.

constraints.name: a[c,
constraints.intent: Each commodity must be in material balance in every period: production must cover use. For each commodity and period, the net amount produced by all the processing activities, together with any direct log supply when the commodity is a log type, must be at least the amount shipped out when the commodity is a final product.

constraints.name: b[m,
constraints.intent: Processing cannot exceed the mill capacity that has been built and is available. For each mill and each period, the total capacity used by all processing activities running that period must not exceed the cumulative capacity that has been built and is live in that period.

constraints.name: phik[t]
constraints.intent: Investment cost is charged on the capacity that is live in each period. For each period, the investment cost equals the capital recovery factor times the total cost of all mill capacity that has been built and is available in that period, valued at each mill's unit investment cost.

constraints.name: phir[t]
constraints.intent: Process cost is the cost of running the processing activities. For each period, the process cost equals the sum over all activities of the activity level run that period valued at its unit process cost.

constraints.name: phix[t]
constraints.intent: Sales revenue comes from the final products shipped to market. For each period, the sales revenue equals the sum over all final products of the amount shipped that period valued at its sales price.

constraints.name: phil[t]
constraints.intent: Cutting cost depends on how much wood is harvested. For each period, the cutting cost equals the unit cutting cost times the total of all log types delivered to industry in that period.

constraints.name: vpos)
constraints.intent: Planting cost depends on how much new forest is established. For each period, the planting cost equals the unit planting cost times the total area of new forest planted in that period across all species and site classes.

constraints.name: phi
constraints.intent: The overall measure of merit aggregates each period's net result, discounted to a common basis. The total discounted net benefit equals the sum over all periods of that period's discount factor times its net benefit, where the net benefit is sales revenue less investment, processing, cutting, and planting costs.

query: We're stress-testing a harvest ceiling on the pulplog side. Suppose we hold total pulplog supply to industry across the whole horizon to no more than 55 (1000 m3/yr units). What does the optimizer return for the discounted benefit?
expected_pyomo: model.pulplog_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.r['pulplogs', te] for te in model.te) <= 55.0)

query: Capital budgeting wants to look at a lid on pulp-mill build-out — total pulp-mill capacity expansion summed over all periods kept to 12. What's the benefit picture with that in place?
expected_pyomo: model.pmill_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.h['pulp-mill', t] for t in model.t) <= 12.0)

params.doc: the discount factor applied to each period's net benefit

params.name: a
params.doc: the input-output matrix: per unit of process activity, the amount of each commodity produced (positive) or consumed (negative)

params.name: b
params.doc: the capacity utilization coefficient: input of mill capacity consumed per unit of each process activity

params.name: avl
params.doc: an availability mask over (period, period) pairs that is nonzero exactly when capacity built in the second period is live and usable in the first period; capacity once built stays available

params.name: iad
params.doc: the initial age distribution: the proportion of each species' existing forest that starts in each age class

params.name: yw
params.doc: the per-hectare yield of each log type from cutting existing forest of a given starting age and species in a given period, in cubic metres per hectare

params.name: yv
params.doc: the per-hectare yield of each log type from managed (newly planted) forest, indexed by planting period, cutting period, species, log type, and site class, in cubic metres per hectare

vars.name: w
vars.doc: the area of existing forest of a given species and site class, starting age and period, that is cut, in thousands of hectares per year; defined only over feasible (initial-age, period) pairs

vars.name: v
vars.doc: the area of managed new forest of a given species and site class, planted in one period and acted on in a later period, in thousands of hectares per year; defined only over feasible (planting-period, period) pairs

vars.name: r
vars.doc: the supply of each log type delivered to industry in each period, in thousands of cubic metres per year

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the activity level of each process in each period, in thousands of cubic metres of input per year

vars.name: h
vars.doc: the capacity expansion built at each mill in each period, in thousands of cubic metres of input per year

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the final shipments of each commodity to market in each period, in thousands of units per year

vars.name: phik
vars.doc: the investment cost incurred in each period, in thousands of US dollars per year

vars.name: phir
vars.doc: the process cost incurred in each period, in thousands of US dollars per year

vars.name: phix
vars.doc: the sales revenue earned in each period, in thousands of US dollars per year

vars.name: phil
vars.doc: the cutting cost incurred in each period, in thousands of US dollars per year

vars.name: phip
vars.doc: the planting cost incurred in each period, in thousands of US dollars per year

vars.name: phi
vars.doc: the total discounted net benefit over the whole horizon

constraints.name: wpos)
constraints.intent: The amount of existing forest that gets cut over the whole horizon cannot exceed how much existing forest there is to begin with. For each species, site class, and starting age class, the total area of that existing stand cut across all the periods where cutting it is admissible must stay within the area of that stand initially present, which is the species' available land scaled by the share of land in that site class and the share of the forest that starts in that age class.

constraints.name: wpos)
constraints.intent: New forest cannot be managed before it exists. For each species, site class, and period, the area of newly planted forest that is acted on in that period cannot exceed the area that has been established by then, namely the existing forest cut in that period plus the new forest planted in earlier periods that becomes available in that period.

constraints.name: wpos)
constraints.intent: The logs delivered to industry must come from what is actually harvested. For each log type and each period, the supply of that log type delivered equals the total yield obtained from cutting, summed over both the managed newly planted forest and the existing forest, across all species, site classes, and source stands feasible for that period, each area multiplied by its per-hectare yield.

constraints.name: te)
constraints.intent: Log supply to industry should never fall over time, so harvests are sustained. For each log type, the supply delivered in a period must be at least the supply delivered in the immediately preceding period. The very first period has no predecessor and so carries no such requirement.

constraints.name: a[c,
constraints.intent: Each commodity must be in material balance in every period: production must cover use. For each commodity and period, the net amount produced by all the processing activities, together with any direct log supply when the commodity is a log type, must be at least the amount shipped out when the commodity is a final product.

constraints.name: b[m,
constraints.intent: Processing cannot exceed the mill capacity that has been built and is available. For each mill and each period, the total capacity used by all processing activities running that period must not exceed the cumulative capacity that has been built and is live in that period.

constraints.name: phik[t]
constraints.intent: Investment cost is charged on the capacity that is live in each period. For each period, the investment cost equals the capital recovery factor times the total cost of all mill capacity that has been built and is available in that period, valued at each mill's unit investment cost.

constraints.name: phir[t]
constraints.intent: Process cost is the cost of running the processing activities. For each period, the process cost equals the sum over all activities of the activity level run that period valued at its unit process cost.

constraints.name: phix[t]
constraints.intent: Sales revenue comes from the final products shipped to market. For each period, the sales revenue equals the sum over all final products of the amount shipped that period valued at its sales price.

constraints.name: phil[t]
constraints.intent: Cutting cost depends on how much wood is harvested. For each period, the cutting cost equals the unit cutting cost times the total of all log types delivered to industry in that period.

constraints.name: vpos)
constraints.intent: Planting cost depends on how much new forest is established. For each period, the planting cost equals the unit planting cost times the total area of new forest planted in that period across all species and site classes.

constraints.name: phi
constraints.intent: The overall measure of merit aggregates each period's net result, discounted to a common basis. The total discounted net benefit equals the sum over all periods of that period's discount factor times its net benefit, where the net benefit is sales revenue less investment, processing, cutting, and planting costs.

query: The whole plan leans awfully hard on the main pulp-from-pulplogs line, and the cumulative run-time on it across the horizon looks excessive. Shouldn't the total throughput we put through that process over all nine model periods stay under 26 (1000 m3 input) so it isn't carrying the entire programme? What's keeping the model from leaning on the other pulping routes instead?
expected_pyomo: model.aggr_pulppl_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.z['pulp-pl', t] for t in model.t) <= 26.0)

model_narrative: We plan the management of a regional forest economy over a sequence of periods. In each period we decide how much existing forest to cut and how much new forest to plant and later manage, by species and site class; how many logs of each type to deliver to industry; how intensely to run each wood-processing activity; how much new mill capacity to build; and how many units of each final product to ship to market. Each period also carries its own bookkeeping figures for investment, processing, cutting and planting costs and for sales revenue. The objective is to maximize the total discounted net benefit over the whole horizon.

sets.name: c
sets.doc: all commodities in the wood economy, spanning both intermediate logs and final products

sets.name: cf
sets.doc: the final products that are sold to market; a subset of the commodities

sets.name: cl
sets.doc: the log types harvested from the forest and fed to industry; a subset of the commodities

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the tree species grown

sets.name: k
sets.doc: site classes describing land quality

sets.name: at
sets.doc: tree age classes of the existing forest, in years

sets.name: u
sets.doc: the initial age class of an existing stand at the start of the horizon; ranges over the same age classes

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the wood-processing activities (processes) that convert log inputs into products

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the productive units (mills) whose capacity must be built and respected

sets.name: te
sets.doc: the extended planning horizon of periods in chronological order; the first member is the opening period

sets.name: t
sets.doc: the model decision horizon of periods, in chronological order; here it coincides with the extended horizon

sets.name: wpos
sets.members: sparse set of feasible (initial-age, period) pairs
sets.doc: the set of feasible (initial age, period) pairs at which an existing stand of that starting age may be cut; only these combinations are admissible

sets.name: vpos
sets.members: sparse set of feasible (planting-period, period) pairs
sets.doc: the set of feasible (planting period, later period) pairs; a stand planted in the first period of the pair can be managed or cut in the second; only these combinations are admissible

query: The plan is leaning the harvest hugely toward pulplogs and barely touching the sawlog side, which feels lopsided for the forest. Shouldn't the total pulplog supply we send to industry over the whole horizon stay within two-point-seven times the total sawlog supply over that same horizon, so the two log streams stay roughly in balance? What's pulling the model so far toward pulplogs?
expected_pyomo: model.pulplog_sawlog_link = Constraint(expr=sum(model.r['pulplogs', te] for te in model.te) <= 2.7 * sum(model.r['sawlogs', te] for te in model.te))

query: The pulp-mill build schedule is full of lumpy spikes — some periods get a big slug of capacity while others get nothing, which is a nightmare for the construction crews. Couldn't we just hold the pulp-mill expansion in every single model period to no more than 1.6 so the build is smoothed out across the horizon? What's pushing the model toward those concentrated bursts?

vars.doc: the planting cost incurred in each period, in thousands of US dollars per year

vars.name: phi
vars.doc: the total discounted net benefit over the whole horizon

constraints.name: wpos)
constraints.intent: The amount of existing forest that gets cut over the whole horizon cannot exceed how much existing forest there is to begin with. For each species, site class, and starting age class, the total area of that existing stand cut across all the periods where cutting it is admissible must stay within the area of that stand initially present, which is the species' available land scaled by the share of land in that site class and the share of the forest that starts in that age class.

constraints.name: wpos)
constraints.intent: New forest cannot be managed before it exists. For each species, site class, and period, the area of newly planted forest that is acted on in that period cannot exceed the area that has been established by then, namely the existing forest cut in that period plus the new forest planted in earlier periods that becomes available in that period.

constraints.name: wpos)
constraints.intent: The logs delivered to industry must come from what is actually harvested. For each log type and each period, the supply of that log type delivered equals the total yield obtained from cutting, summed over both the managed newly planted forest and the existing forest, across all species, site classes, and source stands feasible for that period, each area multiplied by its per-hectare yield.

constraints.name: te)
constraints.intent: Log supply to industry should never fall over time, so harvests are sustained. For each log type, the supply delivered in a period must be at least the supply delivered in the immediately preceding period. The very first period has no predecessor and so carries no such requirement.

constraints.name: a[c,
constraints.intent: Each commodity must be in material balance in every period: production must cover use. For each commodity and period, the net amount produced by all the processing activities, together with any direct log supply when the commodity is a log type, must be at least the amount shipped out when the commodity is a final product.

constraints.name: b[m,
constraints.intent: Processing cannot exceed the mill capacity that has been built and is available. For each mill and each period, the total capacity used by all processing activities running that period must not exceed the cumulative capacity that has been built and is live in that period.

constraints.name: phik[t]
constraints.intent: Investment cost is charged on the capacity that is live in each period. For each period, the investment cost equals the capital recovery factor times the total cost of all mill capacity that has been built and is available in that period, valued at each mill's unit investment cost.

constraints.name: phir[t]
constraints.intent: Process cost is the cost of running the processing activities. For each period, the process cost equals the sum over all activities of the activity level run that period valued at its unit process cost.

constraints.name: phix[t]
constraints.intent: Sales revenue comes from the final products shipped to market. For each period, the sales revenue equals the sum over all final products of the amount shipped that period valued at its sales price.

constraints.name: phil[t]
constraints.intent: Cutting cost depends on how much wood is harvested. For each period, the cutting cost equals the unit cutting cost times the total of all log types delivered to industry in that period.

constraints.name: vpos)
constraints.intent: Planting cost depends on how much new forest is established. For each period, the planting cost equals the unit planting cost times the total area of new forest planted in that period across all species and site classes.

constraints.name: phi
constraints.intent: The overall measure of merit aggregates each period's net result, discounted to a common basis. The total discounted net benefit equals the sum over all periods of that period's discount factor times its net benefit, where the net benefit is sales revenue less investment, processing, cutting, and planting costs.

query: We seem to be over-building the saw mill. Shouldn't total saw-mill capacity expansion across the whole horizon stay under 4.5? What's keeping the model from trimming that?
expected_pyomo: model.sawmill_total_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.h['saw-mill', t] for t in model.t) <= 4.5)
